patch.content:
## Natural Language for Baby Data

Smart Q&A exists because data is only useful if you can ask it questions. The intelligence module generates insights proactively, but parents also need to ask specific questions: "When was her last feed?" or "How has her sleep been this week?" or "What foods correlate with good naps?"

Phase 1 introduced basic query parsing. Phase 2, built across Sessions A and B, expanded to 22 intents with 24 dedicated handlers, transforming Smart Q&A from a search feature into a conversational data interface.

## The 22 Intents

Queries are classified into four categories. Lookback intents retrieve historical data: "How did Ziva sleep last week?" triggers a range summary with duration averages, wake counts, and trend direction. Pattern intents find recurring behaviors: "What’s her feeding pattern on weekdays?" analyzes interval regularity and volume consistency across weekday data. Planning intents project forward: "When should her next vaccine be?" checks the immunization schedule against completed doses. Comparison intents evaluate change: "Is she eating more than last month?" computes period-over-period deltas with statistical context.

Each intent maps to a dedicated handler. The handler doesn’t just return data — it adds context. Ask about sleep duration and you get the raw hours, but also how it compares to her 7-day average, whether the trend is improving or declining, and what the age-appropriate benchmark is. Ask about feeding and you see today’s total alongside the weekly pattern, with any significant deviations highlighted.

## Query Parsing

The temporal query parser understands relative time references: "yesterday," "last week," "the past 3 days," "since Monday," "this month." It also handles domain-specific vocabulary: "feeds" maps to the diet module, "naps" maps to daytime sleep entries, "shots" maps to vaccinations. Compound queries like "how many times did she wake last night?" are decomposed into domain (sleep), metric (wake count), and timeframe (last night = previous sleep session).

The parser uses a keyword-matching approach rather than NLP — no model, no API calls, everything runs locally in the browser. This keeps it fast and offline-capable. The tradeoff is that creative phrasings sometimes miss, but the system handles the 90% case well and degrades gracefully on the rest.

## Graceful Degradation

When a query doesn’t match any intent, the system says so honestly rather than guessing. The response includes a suggestion of similar queries that would work, helping the user learn the system’s vocabulary organically. Partial matches get clarification prompts: "Did you mean sleep duration or sleep quality?" This approach builds trust — parents learn to rely on the answers because the system never fabricates one.
